event: A 37-year-old female was admitted
Patient underwent a Da Vinci-assisted, laparoscopic, one-stage, single-anastomosis DS procedure
An upper gastrointestinal study (UGI) on postoperative day (POD) 2 was negative for a leak
She was discharged on POD 3
She came to the emergency department
Computed tomography (CT) of the abdomen showing free fluid suggestive of a leak or bleeding
She was taken to the operating room (OR) for diagnostic laparoscopy
The patient was kept intubated
The following day she was doing well
She became hypotensive
She was taken back to the OR for exploratory laparotomy with the presumptive diagnosis of bleeding with duodenal stump blowout
She developed acute thrombosis of the left internal jugular vein at a previous central catheter site
The patient was placed on therapeutic Lovenox
Her post-operative course was complicated by hemorrhagic fluid collections seen on CT scan
The patient was successfully discharged to her home
An UGI was negative
She was transferred to the ICU
She was given intravenous piperacillin/tazobactam
Intravenous piperacillin/tazobactam was later changed to cefazolin
Her post-operative course was complicated by atelectasis
The patient developed persistent nausea
She was transfused with 2 units of packed red blood cells (PRBCs) intra-operatively
The patient received 5 units of PRBCs intra-operatively
Bariatric phase 1 diet was started
The patient was discharged with total parenteral nutrition
She was found to be tachycardic
She was found to be anemic
She was found to have a leukocytosis
She was unresponsive to fluids
Her abdominal drain had increased sanguinous output
Her second drain showed frank bile
Frank bile was confirmed with elevated bilirubin
The patient also became tachycardic
The patient developed fatigue
The patient developed severe abdominal pain
The patient was placed on octreotide
There was no pulmonary embolism
Body mass index was 67 kg/m2
Multiple comorbidities included back pain, gastroesophageal reflux disease, obstructive sleep apnea, hyperlipidemia, and hypertension
The patient was sent to intensive care unit (ICU) for further monitoring
She was transferred to step down
She developed acute thrombosis of the left subclavian vein at a previous central catheter site
Home health care was arranged for dressing changes
Home health care was arranged for total parenteral nutrition
The patient received 4 units of fresh frozen plasma intra-operatively
The patient tolerated bariatric phase 1 diet well
Frank bile was confirmed with elevated amylase
Abdominal exam revealed significant distension with guarding
She was found to have hemoperitoneum
Hemoperitoneum was evacuated
Active bleeding source was not identifiable
Complete inspection of the gastroileostomy anastomosis was performed without significant findings
There was no leakage seen with a methylene blue test
An attempt to explore the duodenal stump was made but was unsuccessful due to retraction of the duodenal stump and inflammation around the tissue
Three large 19F Blake drains were placed
The patient underwent laparotomy through a generous right subcostal incision
Upon entry into the peritoneal cavity, a large amount of hemoperitoneum was encountered
Bleeding identified as coming from the gastroduodenal artery which was over-sewn
The duodenal stump was found to have total stump blowout and the staple line was completely undone
A Malecot tube was placed in the duodenum and over-sewn with a purse-string suture
The abdomen was closed and drains were placed
Blood loss was 2500 cc
The anastomosis was tested with methylene blue and air and it showed no leakage
A drain was placed in left upper quadrant
Hemoperitoneum was temporized with packing
Adhesions of the biliopancreatic limb to the anterior abdominal wall resulting in obstruction were taken down
Sleeve gastrectomy was done loosely over a 34 French bougie-sized Edlich tube
The staple line was oversewn
The duodenum was transected 3 cm from the pylorus
The duodenal stump was oversewn with a running absorbable suture
A two-layer hand sewn anastomosis between the duodenum and the ileum was created at the 250 cm mark
The stomach was removed
CT showed inflammation around the duodenal stump site
CT showed perihepatic fluid
The terminal ileum was identified and ran 250 cm proximally up the ileum
The greater omentum was taken from the greater curvature distally 3 cm from the pylorus
A window was created for transection of the duodenum
